interactions.description: Increased risk of bleeding. Combined use significantly increases anticoagulant effect.
Increased risk of gastrointestinal bleeding. NSAIDs enhance anticoagulant effect.
Increased risk of bleeding. NSAIDs potentiate warfarin's effect.
Amiodarone inhibits warfarin metabolism, greatly increasing INR and bleeding risk.
Fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9, increasing warfarin levels and bleeding risk.
Metronidazole potentiates anticoagulant effect of warfarin.
Ciprofloxacin may increase warfarin levels and bleeding risk.
Minor interaction. Atorvastatin may slightly increase warfarin effect.
Complex interaction affecting metabolism of both drugs. Requires close monitoring.
Carbamazepine induces warfarin metabolism, reducing anticoagulant effect.
ACE inhibitors reduce potassium excretion. Combined use may cause hyperkalemia.
Both drugs can increase potassium levels. Monitor serum potassium closely.
NSAIDs may reduce the antihypertensive effect of ACE inhibitors and impair renal function.
NSAIDs may reduce the antihypertensive effect of ACE inhibitors and impair renal function.
Combining ACE inhibitors with ARBs increases risk of hyperkalemia and renal impairment.
Increased risk of lactic acidosis with excessive alcohol consumption.
Cimetidine may increase metformin levels by reducing renal clearance.
Amlodipine increases simvastatin levels. Limit simvastatin dose to 20mg/day with amlodipine.
Minor increase in atorvastatin levels. Usually clinically insignificant.
Both are calcium channel blockers. Additive effects may cause excessive hypotension or bradycardia.
Grapefruit may slightly increase amlodipine levels, potentially enhancing blood pressure lowering.
Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4, greatly increasing statin levels and risk of rhabdomyolysis.
Erythromycin inhibits CYP3A4, increasing statin levels and risk of muscle toxicity.
Strong CYP3A4 inhibitor. Greatly increases statin levels and risk of rhabdomyolysis.
Strong CYP3A4 inhibitor. Greatly increases statin levels and risk of rhabdomyolysis.
Increased risk of rhabdomyolysis and acute kidney injury. Avoid combination.
Clarithromycin inhibits CYP3A4, greatly increasing simvastatin levels. Avoid combination.
Significantly increased risk of rhabdomyolysis. Avoid combination.
Strong CYP3A4 inhibitor. Contraindicated with simvastatin.
Erythromycin greatly increases simvastatin levels. Avoid combination.
Omeprazole reduces the antiplatelet effect of clopidogrel by inhibiting CYP2C19.
Omeprazole may increase diazepam levels by reducing hepatic clearance.
PPIs may reduce methotrexate clearance, increasing toxicity risk.
Omeprazole may slightly increase warfarin effect. Monitor INR.
Combined use increases bleeding risk. Often prescribed together but requires monitoring.
Increased risk of gastrointestinal bleeding with combined antiplatelet and NSAID use.
Increased risk of gastrointestinal bleeding.
SSRIs with antiplatelet drugs increase bleeding risk.
SSRIs with antiplatelet drugs increase bleeding risk.
SSRIs with NSAIDs increase risk of gastrointestinal bleeding.
Increased risk of serotonin syndrome and seizure risk.
Risk of serotonin syndrome with SSRI and triptan combination.
Increased risk of serotonin syndrome.
SSRIs with NSAIDs increase risk of gastrointestinal bleeding.
Beta-blockers may antagonize bronchodilator effects of albuterol.
Beta-blockers may mask symptoms of hypoglycemia (tachycardia, tremor).
Combined use may cause severe bradycardia, heart block, or cardiac arrest.
Additive effects on heart rate. Risk of bradycardia and hypotension.
Fluoxetine may inhibit metoprolol metabolism, increasing its effects.
Amoxicillin may reduce methotrexate clearance, increasing toxicity.
Increased incidence of skin rash with combined use.
May enhance anticoagulant effect. Monitor INR closely.
Calcium reduces absorption of levothyroxine. Separate doses by at least 4 hours.
Iron supplements reduce levothyroxine absorption. Separate by at least 4 hours.
PPIs may reduce levothyroxine absorption due to altered gastric pH.
Combined use significantly increases risk of gastrointestinal ulceration and bleeding.
Combined use increases risk of GI ulceration and bleeding.
Corticosteroids may increase blood glucose, counteracting metformin effect.
ARBs reduce potassium excretion. Combined use may cause hyperkalemia.
NSAIDs may reduce the antihypertensive effect and impair renal function.
Both drugs increase potassium. Monitor serum potassium closely.
Amiodarone increases digoxin levels. Reduce digoxin dose by 50%.
Verapamil increases digoxin levels and additive AV node suppression.
Spironolactone may increase digoxin levels and alter potassium balance.
Combined CNS and respiratory depression. Increased risk of sedation and overdose.
Combined CNS depression increases risk of respiratory depression and death.
Ketoconazole strongly inhibits alprazolam metabolism. Contraindicated.
Itraconazole strongly inhibits alprazolam metabolism. Contraindicated.
Both are opioid analgesics. Additive CNS depression and seizure risk.
Carbamazepine reduces tramadol analgesic effect by inducing metabolism.
Life-threatening hypotension. Contraindicated combination. Wait 24-48h after PDE5 inhibitor.
Life-threatening hypotension. Absolutely contraindicated.
Additive blood pressure lowering. Usually manageable but monitor.
Mutual metabolic induction. Both drugs may have altered effectiveness.
Valproic acid increases free phenytoin levels by displacing protein binding.
Thiazides reduce lithium renal clearance, increasing lithium toxicity risk.
Thiazide-induced hypokalemia may increase digoxin toxicity risk.
